review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

sources.title: Grade 7 - Term 2: The Transatlantic slave trade
Olaudah Equiano, enslaved person and writer, dies
African captives aboard the Amistad revolt
Nat Turner leads a slave insurrection in Southampton, Virginia, USA
William Wilberforce, English philanthropist and champion of the abolishment of the slave trade, is born
British Parliament passes the Abolition of the Slave Trade Act
Slavery abolished throughout the British Empire

slides.title: The Transatlantic Slave Trade
slides.lead: How millions of African people were captured, shipped across the Atlantic Ocean, and forced into slavery - and how that trade was finally ended.

media.alt: A map showing three arrows crossing the Atlantic Ocean between Europe, Africa and the Americas, forming a triangle.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#eaf1f4'/><path d='M40 30 h34 v18 h-34 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M150 60 q26 -10 40 20 q6 40 -18 60 q-30 8 -34 -30 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M30 96 q22 -6 30 24 q-2 34 -26 30 q-18 -30 -4 -54 Z' fill='#a9865a'/><path d='M76 40 q60 8 78 34' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#a)'/><path d='M172 96 q-70 20 -128 12' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M40 96 q10 -30 40 -52' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='150' r='0'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open sensitively. Explain that this lesson deals with a painful history in which real people suffered greatly, and we study it with respect. Set up the four parts of the story we will follow: capture in Africa, the sea voyage called the Middle Passage, slavery in the Americas, and the long struggle to abolish (end) the trade. Note that South Africa has its own connected slave history at the Cape.

slides.title: What was the transatlantic slave trade?
slides.lead: For about 350 years, European traders bought and shipped enslaved African people across the Atlantic Ocean.

blocks.text: From the 1500s to the 1800s, European countries such as Portugal, Britain, France, Spain and the Netherlands captured or bought African people and shipped them to colonies in the Americas. There they were forced to work with no pay and no freedom.

items: 'Transatlantic' means 'across the Atlantic Ocean'.
Historians estimate that about 12 million Africans were shipped across the Atlantic.
Enslaved people were treated as property that could be bought and sold.

slides.notes: Make sure learners understand the word 'enslaved' - a person made a slave by others, not a slave by nature. Prefer 'enslaved person' over 'slave' to keep the person's humanity in view. [SME] The figure of roughly 12 million captives shipped is the widely used estimate from the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database; confirm the exact wording you want pupils to memorise.

slides.title: The triangular trade
slides.lead: Ships sailed a three-sided route between Europe, Africa and the Americas, making a profit at every stop.

items: Europe to Africa: ships carried guns, cloth, alcohol and metal goods to trade for people.
Africa to the Americas: enslaved Africans were packed into ships for the Middle Passage.
Americas to Europe: ships returned full of sugar, cotton, tobacco and coffee.

columns.text: European colonies in the Americas needed huge numbers of workers for their plantations. Enslaved African labour made owners and traders very rich, so the trade grew larger and larger.

media.alt: A triangle diagram with Europe at the top, Africa at the lower right and the Americas at the lower left, arrows running around it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 170' role='img'><rect width='220' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><polygon points='110,24 40,140 180,140' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='110' cy='24' r='7' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='40' cy='140' r='7' fill='#a9865a'/><circle cx='180' cy='140' r='7' fill='#7a8b5a'/><text x='110' y='14' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#333'>Europe</text><text x='30' y='158' font-size='11' fill='#333'>Americas</text><text x='158' y='158' font-size='11' fill='#333'>Africa</text></svg>

slides.notes: Use the triangle diagram to make the route concrete. Stress that human beings formed the cargo on the second leg - the Middle Passage - which the next slides examine. Ask learners why traders thought this system was profitable, and challenge them: profit for whom, and at what human cost?

slides.title: Capture in Africa
slides.lead: People were seized in raids and wars, then marched to the coast and sold.

blocks.text: Most enslaved people came from West and Central Africa. Some were captured in raids or wars, others were kidnapped. They were forced to walk long distances to the coast, often in chains, and held in prisons called 'slave forts' or 'dungeons' at places like Elmina and Goree Island.

items: Families were torn apart and rarely saw each other again.
Captives were sold to European traders in exchange for goods.
They were branded and held in crowded forts before being loaded onto ships.

media.alt: A stone fort on a coastline beside the sea, with a small ship offshore.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#eaf1f4'/><rect y='108' width='240' height='52' fill='#7fa9b8'/><rect x='24' y='150' width='120' height='10' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='30' y='64' width='96' height='48' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='30' y='54' width='16' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='58' y='54' width='16' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='86' y='54' width='16' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='110' y='54' width='16' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='66' y='84' width='16' height='28' fill='#5c4a30'/><path d='M180 96 l10 -30 l10 30 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='189' y='60' width='2' height='36' fill='#5c4a30'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this slide with care and empathy. Emphasise that captives were ordinary people - farmers, parents, children - not criminals. Mention that some African rulers and traders also took part in selling captives, but the demand and the huge scale came from European colonies. Avoid graphic detail; focus on the loss of freedom and family.

slides.title: The Middle Passage
slides.lead: The terrible sea voyage across the Atlantic that could last many weeks.

blocks.text: The 'Middle Passage' was the journey across the Atlantic Ocean, the middle leg of the triangular trade. Hundreds of people were packed tightly below deck with little air, food or water. Disease spread quickly and many did not survive the voyage.

items: Captives were chained together with almost no room to move.
The crossing could take from about five weeks to three months.
Historians estimate that around one in seven captives died during the voyage.

media.alt: A sailing ship on rough waves crossing the ocean.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#dce8ec'/><rect y='104' width='240' height='56' fill='#4f7f92'/><path d='M60 104 h120 l-16 26 h-88 Z' fill='#5c4a30'/><rect x='116' y='40' width='4' height='66' fill='#3d3222'/><path d='M120 46 l44 20 l-44 12 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M116 46 l-40 20 l40 12 Z' fill='#e7ddc9'/><path d='M0 132 q30 -12 60 0 q30 12 60 0 q30 -12 60 0 q30 12 60 0' fill='none' stroke='#eaf1f4' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the emotional heart of the lesson - keep it factual and respectful, not sensational. Explain that survivors of the Middle Passage arrived weakened, frightened and far from home, speaking many different languages. [SME] The 'about one in seven died' figure (roughly 15 percent, near 1.8 million deaths overall) is a commonly cited estimate; verify the exact figure and voyage length you want on record before publishing.

slides.title: Sold at auction
slides.lead: When ships landed, the survivors were sold like goods to the highest bidder.

blocks.text: In ports across Brazil, the Caribbean islands and North America, enslaved Africans were inspected and sold at public auctions. Buyers paid money for people, and families who had survived the voyage together were often separated again on the auction block.

items: Enslaved people were given new names and had no legal rights.
They could be bought, sold, punished or given away by their owners.
Most were sent to plantations that grew sugar, cotton and tobacco.

media.alt: A raised auction platform with a figure standing on it and a small crowd below.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='70' y='96' width='80' height='16' fill='#8a6a3a'/><rect x='78' y='112' width='10' height='24' fill='#5c4a30'/><rect x='132' y='112' width='10' height='24' fill='#5c4a30'/><circle cx='110' cy='72' r='11' fill='#a9865a'/><rect x='103' y='84' width='14' height='14' fill='#a9865a'/><g fill='#7a8b5a'><circle cx='40' cy='120' r='8'/><circle cx='60' cy='124' r='8'/><circle cx='166' cy='122' r='8'/><circle cx='186' cy='120' r='8'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Reinforce the dehumanising nature of slavery: people treated as property to be priced and sold. Connect to fairness and human rights ideas learners already know. Point out that the same crops - sugar, cotton, tobacco - travelled back to Europe on the third leg of the triangle, tying the whole system together.

slides.title: Life on the plantations
slides.lead: Enslaved people worked long, hard hours under harsh control.

blocks.text: On plantations, enslaved people worked from sunrise to sunset with no pay. Overseers used harsh punishment to force people to obey. Despite this cruelty, enslaved communities kept their humanity alive through music, storytelling, religion and by holding on to memories of home.

items: Work included planting and cutting sugar cane, and picking cotton.
Enslaved people had little food, poor housing and no freedom of movement.
African languages, foods and traditions survived and shaped new cultures.

media.alt: Rows of tall plants in a field with a small building in the distance under a hot sun.

slides.notes: Balance the suffering with human dignity and resistance-through-culture: enslaved people were not passive victims. Explain how blended cultures grew in the Americas (music, food, faith) that still shape the world today. This sets up the next slide on active resistance and revolt.

slides.title: Resistance to slavery
slides.lead: Enslaved people resisted in many brave ways, large and small.

blocks.heading: Everyday resistance
blocks.text: Working slowly, breaking tools, running away and keeping African traditions alive.

blocks.heading: Revolts
blocks.text: Uprisings such as the Amistad ship revolt (1839) and Nat Turner's rebellion in Virginia (1831).

blocks.heading: Free communities
blocks.text: Escaped people formed hidden 'maroon' communities and fought to stay free.

slides.notes: Emphasise agency: enslaved people constantly resisted. The Amistad captives seized their ship and eventually won their freedom in a famous court case; Nat Turner led an armed uprising that shook the American South. Both appear in the SAHO dated-events archive. Keep detail age-appropriate and focus on courage and the desire for freedom.

slides.title: Olaudah Equiano tells his story
slides.lead: A formerly enslaved man wrote a famous book describing what he had lived through.

blocks.text: The shrieks of the women, and the groans of the dying, rendered the whole a scene of horror almost inconceivable.

Olaudah Equiano was captured in Africa as a child and survived the Middle Passage. After buying his freedom, he wrote a book about his life. His story helped many people in Britain understand the cruelty of the slave trade and support ending it.

items: His book was one of the first accounts written by a formerly enslaved African.
He travelled and spoke out against the slave trade.
Voices like his helped grow the movement to abolish slavery.

slides.notes: Explain the power of a first-person witness: statistics can feel distant, but Equiano's words make the human reality clear. [SME] The quotation is from Equiano's 1789 Interesting Narrative and is widely reproduced; please verify the exact wording and page reference against a scholarly edition before production, as several slightly different versions circulate.

slides.title: Ending the slave trade
slides.lead: After years of campaigning, laws were passed to abolish (end) the trade and then slavery itself.

blocks.text: 'Abolition' means putting an end to something. Campaigners called abolitionists, together with formerly enslaved people, argued that slavery was wrong and must stop. In Britain, **William Wilberforce** led the fight in Parliament.

items: 1807: Britain passed a law to abolish the slave trade (buying and shipping people).
1833-1834: slavery itself was abolished across the British Empire, including the Cape.
Slavery ended later in other countries - the USA in 1865 and Brazil in 1888.

media.alt: A pair of open, broken chains with a rising sun behind them.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='110' cy='96' r='30' fill='#e8a13a' opacity='0.4'/><g fill='none' stroke='#5c4a30' stroke-width='7'><circle cx='70' cy='84' r='14'/><circle cx='86' cy='100' r='14'/><circle cx='134' cy='84' r='14'/><circle cx='150' cy='100' r='14'/></g><line x1='100' y1='92' x2='120' y2='92' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='10'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the difference between abolishing the trade (1807) and abolishing slavery itself (1833-1834) - a common point of confusion. Note that abolition did not instantly bring equality; formerly enslaved people still faced great hardship. Connect directly to South Africa: slavery at the Cape ended as part of the British Empire's 1834 abolition.

slides.title: Key dates of the transatlantic slave trade

events.label: Transatlantic slave trade begins to grow
Olaudah Equiano publishes his life story
Nat Turner leads a revolt in Virginia
Slavery abolished in the British Empire and at the Cape
Brazil abolishes slavery - the last country in the Americas

slides.notes: Use the timeline to consolidate the whole lesson before the legacy slide. Ask learners to notice that the trade lasted for centuries but ending it took decades of campaigning too. Point out the Cape (1834) date so the story links to their own country's history.

slides.title: The legacy of the slave trade
slides.lead: The effects of the slave trade are still felt around the world today.

blocks.text: The slave trade scattered millions of African people across the Americas and beyond, creating what we call the African diaspora. It brought terrible suffering, but African people also shaped the language, music, food and culture of many countries. Its history of racism and inequality is still being addressed today.

items: At the Cape, enslaved people from Africa and Asia helped shape South African society and the Cape Malay community.
African music and traditions influenced jazz, blues and other world music.
Studying this history helps us stand against slavery and racism today.

slides.notes: Bring the story home to South Africa: the Cape had its own slave trade, and the Slave Lodge in Cape Town remembers this history. Stress the resilience and cultural contribution of enslaved people and their descendants, so learners leave with both an honest account and a sense of human dignity and hope.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: The transatlantic slave trade forced millions of Africans into slavery, but people resisted and it was finally abolished.

blocks.text: We followed the story from **capture** in Africa, across the **Middle Passage**, into **slavery** in the Americas, and through the long struggle for **abolition**. Now show what you have learned.

blocks.question: Explain what the 'triangular trade' was and describe the three legs of the journey.
Why was the Middle Passage such a terrible experience for enslaved Africans?
Describe two ways enslaved people resisted slavery, and explain what 'abolition' means.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as written work or class discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS source-based and paragraph-writing styles. Revisit the key vocabulary: enslaved, transatlantic, Middle Passage, plantation, resistance, abolition. Close by reminding learners that studying this history helps us value freedom and human rights today.
